questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the recommended schedule for PSA measurement and disease-specific history after local therapy for prostate cancer?

options.text: Every 3 months for 3 years, then every 6 months
Every 6 months for a period of 3 years, and then annually
Every 12 months for 5 years, then every 2 years
Every 6 months for 5 years, then annually

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that PSA measurement and disease-specific history are recommended every six months for a period of three years, and then annually.
questions.section_title: 7.3.5. Summary of evidence and recommendations for follow-up after treatment with curative intent
questions.source_text: Most patients who fail treatment for PCa do so within seven years after local therapy [1448]. Patients must be followed more closely during the initial post-treatment period when risk of failure is highest. Prostate-specific antigen measurement and disease-specific history are recommended every six months for a period of three years, and then annually. Whether follow-up should be stopped if PSA remains undetectable (after RP) or stable (after RT) remains an unanswered question, but it seems fair

questions.question: Which of the following symptoms should trigger PSA testing to exclude possible cancer recurrence, according to the guideline?

options.text: Pelvic/skeletal pain, haematuria, progressive LUTS, progressive lower body oedema, progressive bowel complaints, or complaints of fatigue, sarcopenia or unexplained weight loss
Skin rash, joint pain, and vision changes

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly lists these symptoms as triggers for PSA testing to exclude possible cancer recurrence: pelvic/skeletal pain, haematuria, progressive LUTS, progressive lower body oedema, progressive bowel complaints, or complaints of fatigue, sarcopenia or unexplained weight loss.

questions.question: What is the PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after radical prostatectomy (RP)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that the PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after RP is > 0.4 ng/mL.
questions.section_title: 7.4. Follow-up: During first line hormonal treatment (androgen sensitive period)

At the first sign of any detectable PSA
Only after histological confirmation of recurrence

questions.explanation: The guideline strongly recommends that at recurrence, imaging should only be performed if the result will affect treatment planning.

questions.question: How is castrate resistance defined in patients with metastatic prostate cancer?

options.text: Rising PSA levels during ADT treatment
PCa progression despite a testosterone level < 50ng/dL
Development of new metastatic lesions on imaging
Failure to achieve initial PSA response to treatment

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that castrate resistance is defined as 'PCa progression despite a testosterone level < 50ng/dL'. The other options describe scenarios that may occur during ADT but are not the formal definition of castrate resistance.
questions.source_text: Androgen deprivation therapy is used in various situations: combined with RT for localised or locally advanced disease for relapse after a local treatment, or in the presence of metastatic disease in combination with other treatments. All these situations are based on the benefits of testosterone blockage or suppression either by drugs (LHRH agonists or antagonists) or orchidectomy. In the majority of patients with metastatic PCa, castrate resistance will develop, which is defined as PCa progres

questions.question: Regular clinical follow-up in patients on ADT is mandatory because:

options.text: Laboratory tests alone are insufficient to detect all treatment-related complaints
Imaging studies cannot reliably assess treatment response in prostate cancer
PSA monitoring needs to be performed at each clinical visit
Imaging or laboratory tests can fully replace clinical assessment

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'regular clinical follow-up is mandatory and cannot be replaced by imaging or laboratory tests alone' to detect disease- and treatment-related complaints. Option D is the opposite of what the text states, making it clearly incorrect.

questions.question: How frequently should patients be evaluated after initiation of ADT?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that after the initiation of ADT, patients should be evaluated every three to six months, though this must be individualised.
questions.section_title: 7.4.3. General follow-up of men on hormonal therapy
questions.source_text: The main objectives of follow-up in patients receiving ADT are to ensure treatment compliance, to monitor treatment response, to detect and manage side effects early, and to guide treatment at the time point of clinical progression. After the initiation of ADT, it is recommended that patients be evaluated every three to six months. This must be individualised, and each patient should be advised to contact his physician in the event of troublesome symptoms. This is even more important for patient

questions.question: What is the recommended follow-up frequency for the first three months when ADT is combined with an ARPI?
questions.explanation: The guideline specifies that for patients receiving a combination of ADT and ARPI, the frequency of follow-up is monthly for the first three months.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of patients experience breakthrough for the testosterone threshold <20 ng/dL?
questions.explanation: The text states that breakthrough rates for the <20 ng/dL threshold were found to be more frequent (41.3%).
questions.source_text: Testosterone monitoring should be considered standard clinical practice in men on ADT. Many men receiving medical castration will achieve a castrate testosterone level (< 20ng/dL), and most will achieve a testosterone level (< 50ng/dL). questions.question: What is the recommended management if a castrate testosterone level is not reached on ADT?

options.text: Increase the dose of current medication and recheck after 1 month
Perform immediate prostate biopsy to assess tumor progression
Switch to another agonist or antagonist or to an orchiectomy

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that if a castrate testosterone level is not reached, switching to another agonist or antagonist or to an orchiectomy should be considered.

questions.question: How frequently should transaminase levels be checked in all men receiving combined ADT?

options.text: At least once every six months

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'transaminase levels should be checked at least once yearly in all men receiving combined ADT.'
questions.source_text: Liver function tests will detect treatment toxicity this is especially applicable for NSAA abiraterone acetate and novel AR antagonists. options.text: After the first year of treatment
During the first six months after treatment initiation
During the entire duration of treatment

questions.explanation: The text states that 'liver function abnormalities were most commonly observed during this period in large clinical trials,' referring to the first six months after treatment initiation.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the significance of haemoglobin decline timing in patients receiving ADT?

options.text: An early decrease at 3 months predicts better survival, while a decrease beyond 6 months is associated with poor outcome
A decrease at any time point consistently indicates disease progression
Declines occurring before 3 months are the strongest predictor of poor survival
The timing of haemoglobin decline has no prognostic significance

questions.explanation: The text states that 'an early decrease in haemoglobin three months after ADT initiation predicted better survival, whereas a decrease beyond six months was associated with poor outcome in the SPCG-5 population'.
questions.source_text: Estimated glomerular filtration rate monitoring is good clinical practice because an increase may be linked to ureteral obstruction or bladder retention. A decline in haemoglobin is a known side effect of ADT. A significant decline after three months of ADT is independently associated with shorter progression-free and OS rates and might explain significant fatigue, although other causes should be considered [1461]. Anaemia is often multifactorial and other possible aetiologies should be excluded

questions.question: What is the clinical significance of a significant decline in haemoglobin after three months of ADT?

options.text: It is independently associated with shorter progression-free and overall survival rates
It always indicates tumour progression requiring immediate treatment modification
It is primarily caused by bone marrow metastasis
It predicts excellent response to ADT and long-term remission

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that 'a significant decline after three months of ADT is independently associated with shorter progression-free and OS rates'.

questions.question: What baseline screening tests are recommended for all patients before starting androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?

options.text: Fasting glucose and HbA1c to screen for diabetes
Liver function tests and complete blood count

questions.explanation: The guideline states that all patients should be screened for diabetes by checking fasting glucose and HbA1c at baseline and routinely, in addition to checking blood lipid levels.
questions.source_text: The most severe complications of androgen suppression are metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular morbidity, mental health problems and bone resorption (see Section 8.2.5). All patients should be screened for diabetes by checking fasting glucose and HbA1c (at baseline and routinely) in addition to checking blood lipid levels. questions.question: Which cardiac investigation is mandatory before initiating androgen deprivation therapy in all men?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that an ECG should be done prior to starting ADT. ECHO is mentioned as additional testing only in men with history of cardiovascular disease depending on the combination drug planned.

questions.question: According to the guidelines, which imaging modality should be used for routine bone monitoring at the start of androgen deprivation therapy (ADT)?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'routine bone monitoring for osteoporosis should be performed at the start of ADT using dual emission X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan.' DEXA scan is the only imaging modality mentioned for bone monitoring in this context.
questions.source_text: Androgen deprivation therapy increases the risk of osteoporosis. Administration of ADT for more than a year, as compared to less than one year, showed a higher risk of osteoporosis (HR: 1.77 and 1.38, respectively) [1468]. questions.question: Which patient group has been shown to have the highest incidence of fractures according to the guideline?

options.text: Men with low baseline vitamin D levels
Older men and men on additional novel ARPI medication
Men receiving ADT without any additional therapy

questions.explanation: The text states there is a 'near doubling of the risk of fractures when using ADT depending on patients' age and duration and type of ADT with the highest incidence in older men and men on additional novel ARPI medication.' This indicates that older men and those on novel ARPI medications have the highest fracture incidence.

questions.question: According to the guideline, how does ADT affect the risk of depression in men?

options.text: It slightly increases the risk but the data is inconclusive
It reduces the risk of depression compared to the general population
Men on ADT are three times more likely to report depression
It has no significant effect on mental health

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'men on ADT are three times more likely to report depression.' This statistic highlights why attention to mental health should be an integral part of the follow-up scheme for these patients.

questions.source_text: Lifestyle (e.g. diet, exercise, smoking status and so on) affects QoL and potentially outcome [1475]. questions.question: What proportion of couples cease sexual activity after a man starts ADT?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that ADT 'leads to sexual dysfunction, causing >80% of couples to cease sexual activity completely.' The text also notes that this affects both patients and their partners, recommending that couple counselling should be considered.

questions.question: What percentage of men on ADT may experience temporary testosterone surges (testosterone > 50 ng/dL) during long-term treatment?
questions.explanation: The guideline text states that up to 24% of men may experience temporary testosterone surges (testosterone > 50 ng/dL) during long-term treatment, referred to as 'acute on-chronic effect' or 'breakthrough response'.

questions.source_text: Patients under ADT require regular follow-up, including monitoring of serum testosterone, creatinine, liver function and metabolic parameters at three-to six-month intervals. questions.question: According to the guidelines, liver function tests (transaminase levels) should be monitored at which frequency in men receiving combined ADT?

options.text: At least once every three months
At least once yearly with more frequent monitoring in the first six months
Only at baseline before treatment initiation

questions.explanation: The guideline text states that transaminase levels should be checked at least once yearly in all men receiving combined ADT, with more frequent monitoring warranted in the first six months after treatment initiation as liver function abnormalities were most commonly observed during this period.

questions.question: According to the guideline, when should imaging be considered in prostate cancer follow-up?

options.text: At every routine follow-up visit regardless of PSA levels
When PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis
Only when PSA exceeds 10ng/mL to reduce unnecessary imaging
Before starting any form of androgen deprivation therapy

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis.
questions.source_text: Prostate-specific antigen is a key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised PCa. Imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis.

questions.question: What role does prostate-specific antigen play according to this guideline?

options.text: A primary screening tool for detecting prostate cancer in asymptomatic men
A key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised prostate cancer
A definitive diagnostic test that replaces the need for biopsy
A measure of response to radiation therapy only

questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that PSA is a key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised prostate cancer.
questions.source_text: Prostate-specific antigen is a key marker for following the course of androgen-sensitive non-metastasised PCa. Imaging should be considered when PSA is rising > 2ng/mL or in case of symptoms suggestive of metastasis.

questions.question: What imaging is currently recommended for restaging when PSA progression suggests castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) and treatment modification is considered?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that 'when PSA progression suggests CRPC status and treatment modification is considered, imaging, by means of a bone and CT scan, is currently recommended for restaging.'
questions.source_text: The choice of imaging modality is between PSMA-PET/CT, which offers higher sensitivity, and conventional imaging (CI) with CT or MRI and bone scan on which almost all clinical studies and guideline recommendations are based (see Section 5.8). With its higher sensitivity, next-generation imaging may detect progression earlier. options.text: Imaging is reserved for symptomatic patients only

questions.explanation: The guideline indicates that 'Imaging should be scheduled regularly, including in asymptomatic patients with stable PSA,' noting that the earlier recommendation that such patients do not require further imaging may no longer hold true.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what PSA threshold during androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) suggests a likely prolonged response and allows for follow-up visits every 3-6 months?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'a PSA decline to < 2.4ng/mL suggests a likely prolonged response' and follow-up visits can be scheduled every three to six months.
questions.source_text: In men on ADT alone, a PSA decline to < 2.4ng/mL suggests a likely prolonged response and follow-up visits can be scheduled every three to six months, provided the patient is asymptomatic or clinically improving. This applies to men on ADT monotherapy as well as after ADT plus docetaxel [1219]. Depending on symptoms and risk assessment, more frequent visits may be indicated. Treatment response may be evaluated based on a change in serum PSA level [1218,1219] and bone and CT scan, al

questions.question: What is the mandatory assessment required when a rising PSA is detected during ADT to define castration-resistant prostate cancer (CRPC) status?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'a rising PSA should prompt assessment of testosterone level, which is mandatory to define CRPC status, as well as restaging using imaging.'

questions.question: The bone scan 'flare' phenomenon in metastatic prostate cancer:

options.text: Can be distinguished from true progression on a single bone scan by increased intensity of tracer uptake
Is defined by treatment-induced demasking of previously invisible metastases and represents a favourable response when observed within 8-12 weeks of treatment initiation
Indicates treatment failure and requires immediate change of systemic therapy
Can only be reliably detected using PET/CT imaging

questions.explanation: The text states the flare phenomenon is defined by treatment-induced demasking of earlier invisible metastases and represents a favourable response when observed within eight to twelve weeks of treatment initiation. It also states that differentiation between progression and flare requires repeated bone scans.
questions.section_title: 7.4.5.b. Imaging as a marker of response in metastatic PCa
questions.source_text: Treatment response in soft-tissue metastases can be assessed by morphological imaging methods using the Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours (RECIST) criteria. However, these criteria cannot be used for bone metastases in which response assessment is difficult [1483,1484]. When bone scan is used to follow bone metastases, a quantitative estimation of tracer uptake at bone scan can be obtained through automated methods such as the Bone Scan Index [1485]. questions.question: According to the guidelines, what proportion of men with PSA <2ng/mL showed clinical progression in the CHAARTED trial?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'one in eight men with a PSA <2ng/mL showed clinical progression' in the CHAARTED trial. This supports the recommendation that men with metastasised PCa on ADT should be followed with regular imaging even in the absence of PSA rise.

questions.question: What is the time threshold used to define 'long-term consequences' in Section 8.2 of the guideline chapter?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that Section 8.2 summarises long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa.
questions.source_text: This chapter is presented in two parts. The first part of this chapter (Section 8.2) summarises long-term consequences (≥ twelve months) of therapies for PCa. Based on two SRs. The second part of this chapter (Section 8.3) provides evidence-based recommendations for supporting patients when selecting primary treatment options for localised disease and supportive interventions aimed at improving disease-specific QoL across all stages of disease.

questions.question: According to the guideline text, which of the following is specifically mentioned as part of the multidisciplinary team for prostate cancer care?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that a multidisciplinary team includes 'urologists, medical oncologists, radiation oncologists, oncology nurses, behavioural practitioners and many others, including fellow patients.' Fellow patients are specifically mentioned as part of the team.

options.text: Continence is considerably superior in active surveillance compared to radical prostatectomy even after extended periods
Continence outcomes are equivalent between active surveillance and radical prostatectomy after five years
Active surveillance leads to worse continence outcomes compared to radiotherapy
There is no significant difference in continence between any treatment modalities

questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that 'in preservation of continence, there is a clear advantage for AS over active treatment, particularly to RP' and that 'even after extended periods, continence is still considerably superior in AS to that in RP.'

questions.question: According to the comparative data between RALP and RRP, what were the continence rates at twelve months in the prospective controlled non-randomised trial?
questions.explanation: The text states that the prospective trial demonstrated at twelve months 21.3% were incontinent after RALP and 20.2% after RRP. The continence rates of 89-100% and 80-97% refer to mean continence rates from systematic reviews, not from the prospective trial. The ED rates of 70.4% and 74.7% are for erectile dysfunction, not continence.

questions.question: What is reported as the most common postoperative complication following radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'The most common postoperative complication is ED' (erectile dysfunction). While long-term incontinence is described as the second most commonly occurring complication, and inguinal hernia and bladder neck contracture are mentioned as related issues, erectile dysfunction is identified as the most common.

options.text: IMRT has similar bowel toxicity to 3D-CRT
IMRT causes more bowel toxicity than 3D-CRT
IMRT demonstrates less bowel toxicity than 3D-CRT
IMRT has been replaced by 3D-CRT in modern practice

questions.question: According to the systematic review and meta-analysis, what was found regarding urinary incontinence at one year when comparing whole-gland cryotherapy to radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: Cryotherapy had significantly higher incontinence rates than RP
Cryotherapy had significantly lower incontinence rates than RP, but the difference decreased with longer follow-up
Cryotherapy had identical incontinence rates to RP at one year
Cryotherapy had no significant difference compared to RP in terms of incontinence

questions.explanation: The text states that 'the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up' [853].
questions.source_text: An SR and meta-analysis produced evidence that the rate of urinary incontinence at one year was lower for whole-gland cryotherapy than for RP, but the size of the difference decreased with longer follow-up [853]. No significant difference was seen between cryotherapy versus EBRT in terms of urinary incontinence at one year (< 1%); cryotherapy had a similar ED rate (range 0–40%) to RP at one year. questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of people undergoing androgen-deprivation therapy (ADT) experience cessation of sexual activity?
questions.explanation: The text states that cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93%.
questions.source_text: Cessation of sexual activity is very common in people undergoing ADT, affecting up to 93% [1531]. Androgen-deprivation therapy reduces both libido and the ability to gain and maintain erections. The management of acquired ED is mostly non-specific [1532]. Using a specific nonvalidated questionnaire, bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months [1533]. questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that bicalutamide monotherapy showed a significant advantage over castration in the domains of physical capacity and sexual interest (but not sexual function) at twelve months.

questions.question: According to the guideline text, which statement about hot flushes during ADT is correct?

options.text: They affect approximately 20-30% of men on ADT
They usually appear about 6 months after starting ADT
They have a significant impact on quality of life

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT with prevalence estimated between 44-80%, appear three months after starting ADT (not 6 months), persist long-term, and have a significant impact on QoL.
questions.source_text: Hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT (prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT) [1531]. They appear three months after starting ADT, usually persist long-term and have a significant impact on QoL. Serotonin re-uptake inhibitors (e.g. venlafaxine or sertraline) appear to be less effective in reducing hot flushes than hormonal therapies based on a prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, 75 mg daily, with medroxyprogesterone, 20 mg daily, or cyproterone acetate, 100 mg daily [1536].

questions.question: Based on the prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, medroxyprogesterone, and cyproterone acetate for hot flushes, which statement is supported by the data?

options.text: Venlafaxine showed the best efficacy in reducing hot flushes
Cyproterone acetate demonstrated superior efficacy compared to venlafaxine
All three treatments showed equivalent efficacy

questions.explanation: The study showed median daily hot-flush score reductions of -47.2% for venlafaxine, -94.5% for cyproterone acetate, and -83.7% for medroxyprogesterone, clearly demonstrating that cyproterone acetate was superior to venlafaxine.
questions.source_text: Hot flushes are a common side effect of ADT (prevalence estimated between 44–80% of men on ADT) [1531]. They appear three months after starting ADT, usually persist long-term and have a significant impact on QoL. Serotonin re-uptake inhibitors (e.g. venlafaxine or sertraline) appear to be less effective in reducing hot flushes than hormonal therapies based on a prospective RCT comparing venlafaxine, 75 mg daily, with medroxyprogesterone, 20 mg daily, or cyproterone acetate, 100 mg daily [1536].

questions.question: According to the guideline, what was the incidence of non-metastatic fractures in patients receiving enzalutamide, apalutamide, or darolutamide in combination with ADT?
questions.explanation: The text states that the incidence of non-metastatic fractures was 242 (4%) in the enzalutamide/apalutamide/darolutamide group compared to 107 (2%) in the control group.
questions.source_text: ADT leads to increased bone turnover and decreased bone mineral density (BMD) over time, which significantly elevates the risk of fractures. The relative risk of fractures increases by up to 45% with long-term ADT [1541]. Severe fractures in men are associated with increased mortality [1542], underscoring the importance of early identification of high-risk patients and timely preventive measures. Insulin sensitivity remains unchanged throughout treatment

questions.question: What is the best protective measure for fatigue developing as a side effect of ADT (Androgen Deprivation Therapy)?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure' for fatigue related to ADT. Iron supplementation, blood transfusion, and vitamin B12 injection are not mentioned as protective measures for ADT-related fatigue.
questions.source_text: Fatigue often develops as a side effect of ADT, for which regular exercise appears to be the best protective measure. questions.question: When a patient reports clinically significant fatigue during ADT, what should be prompted according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The text directly states that 'reporting clinically significant fatigue is associated with severe psychological distress and should prompt screening for anxiety and/or depression.' Testosterone supplementation, discontinuation of ADT, and bone density screening are not mentioned as responses to clinically significant fatigue.

questions.question: According to the guideline, castration is associated with an increased risk of which cardiovascular complication?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that castration is associated with an increased risk of stroke.
questions.source_text: Castration also appears to be associated with an increased risk of stroke [1580], and is suspected to be associated with an increased risk for depression and cognitive decline such as Alzheimer disease [1581].

From a seated position, at usual pace, over six meters

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that gait speed is measured from a standing start, at usual pace, generally over six meters.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is co-morbidity described as in localized prostate cancer treated with radical prostatectomy?

options.text: A major predictor of non-cancer-specific death
A minor factor compared to age

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Co-morbidity is a major predictor of non-cancer-specific death in localised PCa treated with RP and is more important than age.'
questions.source_text: Co-morbidity is a major predictor of non-cancer-specific death in localised PCa treated with RP and is more important than age [576,577]. Ten years after watchful waiting for PCa, most men with a high co-morbidity score had died from competing causes, irrespective of age or tumour aggressiveness [576]. Measures for co-morbidity include: Cumulative Illness Score Rating-Geriatrics (CISR-G) [578,579] (Table 6.1.2) and Charlson Co-morbidity Index (CCI) [580].

questions.question: What was the outcome for most men with a high co-morbidity score 10 years after watchful waiting for prostate cancer, according to the guideline?

options.text: They died from competing causes regardless of age or tumour aggressiveness
Their survival was determined primarily by tumour aggressiveness
They had equivalent outcomes to men with low co-morbidity scores

questions.explanation: The text directly states that 'Ten years after watchful waiting for PCa, most men with a high co-morbidity score had died from competing causes, irrespective of age or tumour aggressiveness.'

questions.question: According to the guideline, what percentage of body weight loss over the previous three months defines severe malnutrition?
questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that severe malnutrition corresponds to >10% weight loss.
questions.source_text: Malnutrition can be estimated from body weight during the previous three months (good nutritional status < 5% weight loss; risk of malnutrition: 5–10% weight loss; severe malnutrition: > 10% weight loss) [581].

questions.question: What weight loss range is considered a risk of malnutrition according to the same guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline identifies a risk of malnutrition when weight loss is between 5% and 10%.

Proceed directly with surgery without further evaluation

questions.question: Which of the following tools are used to measure overall physical functioning?

options.text: Barthel Index and Functional Independence Measure

questions.explanation: According to the guideline, Karnofsky score and ECOG scores are the measures for overall physical functioning. ADL and IADL measure dependence in daily activities, not overall physical functioning.

IADL measures activities requiring higher cognition and judgement
IADL requires assistance from caregivers at all times
IADL is assessed using muscle strength testing

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that IADL involves activities requiring higher cognition and judgement, distinguishing it from ADL which covers basic activities of daily living.

Equal importance should be given to patient values, preferences and disease characteristics
Patient preferences should only be considered if disease characteristics are inconclusive
Patient values are secondary to laboratory findings

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that in older and frail patients, aspects such as values, preferences, QoL and functional ability should be given equal importance to disease characteristics during the decision-making process.

Family involvement should be avoided to preserve patient autonomy

All patients over 70 years regardless of G8 score

questions.explanation: The guideline states that both healthy patients with a G8 score > 14 AND vulnerable patients with reversible impairment after resolution of their geriatric problems should receive the same treatment as younger patients.

options.text: PSA testing and digital rectal examination
Geriatric 8, mini-COG and Clinical Frailty Scale

Full specialist geriatric evaluation before any treatment decision

questions.explanation: The guidelines weakly recommend offering adapted treatment or watchful waiting to patients with irreversible impairment. Standard treatment is only considered for patients with reversible impairments after resolution of geriatric problems.

questions.question: What are the ten-year cancer-specific survival (CSS) rates reported for patients on active surveillance according to the guideline?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that long-term oncological outcomes of patients on AS are very favourable, with ten-year CSS rates of 98.1-100%.

questions.question: What is the primary difference in treatment intent between active surveillance and watchful waiting according to the EAU guidelines?

options.text: Active surveillance aims for curative intent while watchful waiting is palliative
Watchful waiting aims for curative intent while active surveillance is palliative
Both strategies aim for palliative treatment intent
Both strategies aim for curative treatment intent

questions.explanation: According to the guidelines table, active surveillance has curative treatment intent with the aim of minimising treatment-related toxicity without compromising survival, while watchful waiting has palliative treatment intent, maintaining quality of life by delaying side effects of palliative androgen deprivation therapy.

questions.explanation: The guidelines explicitly state that in the ProtecT trial, prostate cancer-related death was 3% at 15 years compared to death from any cause in 21.7% of patients, demonstrating that death from other causes is significantly more likely than death from prostate cancer in this population.

Eradication of cancer while preserving pelvic organ function whenever possible

questions.explanation: The systematic review of 60 studies with over 400,000 patients found an emergency room visit rate of 12% at 30 days postoperatively. The 4% figure refers to hospital readmission rate, 11% refers to neuropathy risk, and 21.3% refers to incontinence rate at 12 months.

options.text: Erectile dysfunction was observed in 74.7% after RARP and 70.4% after RRP
Erectile dysfunction was observed in 70.4% after RARP and 74.7% after RRP with adjusted OR of 0.81 (95% CI: 0.66-0.98)
Erectile dysfunction rates were identical between both procedures at 21.3%
Erectile dysfunction was higher after RARP with adjusted OR of 1.08

questions.explanation: The prospective controlled non-RCT showed erectile dysfunction in 70.4% after RARP versus 74.7% after RRP, with adjusted OR of 0.81 (95% CI: 0.66-0.98), indicating RARP had better erectile function outcomes. The 21.3% figure refers to incontinence, not erectile dysfunction.

questions.question: In the PROG 95-09 trial comparing 70.2 Gy versus 79.2 Gy (including proton boost), what was the primary outcome that showed significant improvement with dose escalation?
questions.explanation: The PROG 95-09 trial showed significant improvement in ASTRO biochemical failure with dose escalation. For all patients: 32% biochemical failure at 70.2Gy vs 17% at 79.2Gy (p<0.0001). For low-risk patients: 28% vs 7% (p<0.0001). No significant differences in OS were reported.

Patients who have failed initial hormone therapy

questions.question: According to EAU guidelines, which of the following represents the appropriate patient selection criteria for radical prostatectomy?

options.text: Low tumour volume and absence of fixed pelvic wall disease or urethral sphincter invasion
High PSA level and Gleason score greater than 8
Presence of regional lymph node metastases with resectable disease
Young age and good performance status regardless of tumour volume

questions.explanation: The guidelines state that radical prostatectomy is a standard option in selected patients with low tumour volume, provided the tumour is not fixed to the pelvic wall and there is no invasion of the urethral sphincter. The other options include criteria not mentioned in the text.
questions.source_text: Radical prostatectomy is a standard option in selected patients with a low tumour volume, provided that the tumour is not fixed to the pelvic wall or there is no invasion of the urethral sphincter. Patients should be aware preoperatively that surgery may be part of multimodal treatment, with adjuvant SRT or ADT; however, almost half of patients will be free from recurrence without multimodal treatment at five years [900,901]. Neoadjuvant therapy using ADT is not indicated [902].

Neoadjuvant ADT is preferred over adjuvant therapy
Neoadjuvant ADT can be considered in selected high-risk patients

questions.explanation: The guidelines explicitly state that neoadjuvant therapy using ADT is not indicated [902]. This is a clear recommendation against this approach, not merely a consideration for selected cases.

questions.explanation: The guidelines state that long-term ADT (at least two to three years) is currently recommended for high-risk localised prostate cancer, as EBRT plus short-term ADT did not improve overall survival in several studies.
questions.source_text: For high-risk localised PCa, a combined modality approach should be used consisting of IMRT/VMAT plus long-term ADT. The duration of ADT must take into account PS, comorbidities and the number of poor prognostic factors. It is important to recognise that in several studies, EBRT plus short-term ADT did not improve OS in high-risk localised PCa and long-term ADT (at least two to three years) is currently recommended for these patients [794,795,803]. Moderate HFX is an option in high-risk patients

questions.question: What was the main finding of the single-centre RCT comparing prostate-only RT (PORT) versus whole pelvic RT (WPRT) in high-risk localised and locally advanced cN0 prostate cancer?

options.text: WPRT significantly improved OS without increasing toxicity, establishing it as standard of care
WPRT significantly improved distant metastasis-free survival and disease-free survival, but with significantly higher late GU toxicity

questions.explanation: The trial showed WPRT significantly improved distant MFS (95.9% vs 89.2%, HR: 0.35) and DFS (89.5% vs 77.2%), but with significantly higher late GU ≥2 effects (17.7% vs 7.5%). The findings were insufficient to define a change in practice due to trial size limitations.

In contrast to these studies, a propensity score-matched retrospective analysis of two cohorts of 366 pT3 and/or R1 patients found that compared to SRT at a PSA between 0.1 and 0.5ng/mL, ART given at an undetectable PSA (< 0.1ng/mL) improved all three endpoints: BCR, MFS and

questions.question: Why did the TAX3501 trial end prematurely?
questions.explanation: The TAX3501 trial comparing leuprolide with and without docetaxel ended prematurely due to poor accrual.
questions.section_title: 6.3.5.e. Adjuvant systemic therapy in N0 disease
questions.source_text: The TAX3501 trial comparing the role of leuprolide (18 months) with and without docetaxel (six cycles) ended prematurely due to poor accrual. A phase III RCT comparing adjuvant docetaxel against surveillance after RP for locally advanced PCa showed that adjuvant docetaxel did not confer any oncological benefit [965]. Consequently, adjuvant chemotherapy after RP should only be considered in a clinical trial [966].

options.text: Did not confer any oncological benefit

Men with high-volume nodal disease and low-grade tumors

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what is the recommendation regarding adjuvant androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) for pN0 prostate cancer patients?

options.text: Offer adjuvant ADT to all pN0 patients
Do not prescribe adjuvant ADT to pN0 patients
Offer adjuvant ADT only to pN0 patients with positive margins
Prescribe adjuvant ADT as first-line treatment for pN0 patients

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states 'Do not prescribe adjuvant androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) to pN0 patients' with a Strong rating. This is a clear recommendation against adjuvant ADT in pN0 disease regardless of other pathological features.
questions.section_title: 6.3.5.g. Recommendations for adjuvant treatment for pN0 and pN1 disease after radical prostatectomy*
questions.source_text: Recommendations Strength rating Do not prescribe adjuvant androgen deprivation therapy (ADT) to pN0 patients. Strong In pN0 patients with ISUP GG 4–5 and pT3 ± positive margins, offer adjuvant intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT)/volumetric modulated arc therapy (VMAT) plus image-guided radiation therapy (IGRT). Weak In pN1 patients, after an extended lymph node dissection (eLND), discuss three management options, based on nodal involvement characteristics: Offer adjuvant ADT.

questions.explanation: The guidelines recommend offering adjuvant IMRT/VMAT plus IGRT to pN0 patients with ISUP GG 4-5 and pT3 ± positive margins. This is rated as a Weak recommendation and specifically excludes adjuvant ADT, which should not be prescribed to pN0 patients.

questions.question: What is the definition of PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: PSA ≥ 0.1 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery
PSA ≥ 0.2 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery
PSA ≥ 0.1 ng/mL within two weeks of surgery
PSA ≥ 0.5 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery

questions.explanation: According to the text, PSA persistence is defined as a PSA ≥ 0.1 ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery.
questions.source_text: Detectable PSA levels after RP may result from persistent local disease, pre-existing metastases, or residual benign prostate tissue. PSA persistence, defined as a PSA ≥ 0.1ng/mL within four to eight weeks of surgery, is observed in 5–20% of patients [976,977]. 77% had PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, and nearly all remained detectable on repeat testing

questions.explanation: The text states that among patients tested within three weeks of RP, 77% had PSA > 0.1 ng/mL, but only 21% remained detectable on repeat testing, highlighting the importance of timing in PSA interpretation.

questions.source_text: A meta-analysis of consecutive patient cohorts reported that persistent PSA was more likely when risk factors such as high D’Amico risk, ISUP GG 4-5, pT stage ≥ 8 and presence of extraprostatic extension, seminal vesicle invasion, lymph node involvement and positive margin were present [980]. questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly lists seminal vesicle invasion, positive margins, and ISUP GG 4-5 among the risk factors for persistent PSA. Perineural invasion, while a known pathological finding in prostate cancer, is not mentioned in the provided text as a predictor of persistent PSA.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the hazard ratio for metastasis in patients with persistent PSA after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The text states that in multivariable Cox regression models, persistent PSA represented an independent predictor for metastasis with HR: 3.59 (p < 0.001).
questions.section_title: 6.3.6.c. Natural history of persistently elevated PSA after RP
questions.source_text: Two SRs addressing persistent PSA confirmed a strong correlation of PSA persistence with poor oncologic outcomes [976,977]. In a meta-analysis of retrospective cohorts, patients with persistent PSA (≥ 0.1 ng/mL) had worse BCR-free, metastasis-free and cancer-specific survival [980]. A retrospective study (n = 11,605) showed that persistent PSA is prognostic of an increased risk of metastasis and death [984]. questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'Patients with persistent PSA levels ≥ 1 ng/ml had higher all-cause and PCa-specific mortality risk compared to those with PSA levels < 1 ng/mL.'

questions.explanation: After 1:1 propensity score matching, the study showed 10-year cancer-specific survival rates of 94% for patients treated with SRT versus 82% for those who did not receive radiotherapy (p < 0.01).

questions.question: According to the guideline text, what do available data suggest about patients with PSA persistence after radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: They have worse outcomes compared to patients without PSA persistence
They have better outcomes with standard salvage therapy alone
They have outcomes comparable to patients with PSA recurrence
They show no significant difference in long-term survival rates

questions.explanation: The text states that 'patients with PSA persistence after RP have worse outcomes,' indicating that PSA persistence is associated with poorer prognosis.
questions.source_text: The available data suggest that patients with PSA persistence after RP have worse outcomes and serve to benefit most from early aggressive multimodality treatment, however, the lack of prospective RCTs makes firm recommendations difficult.

questions.question: What is cited as the main limitation preventing firm recommendations for treatment of PSA persistence after RP?

options.text: The lack of prospective randomized controlled trials (RCTs)
Insufficient imaging modalities to detect persistent disease
Variability in PSA testing methods across centers
Inadequate surgeon experience with the RP procedure

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'the lack of prospective RCTs makes firm recommendations difficult,' highlighting this as the primary barrier to establishing definitive treatment guidelines.

questions.question: According to the guidelines, when should a PSMA PET/CT scan be offered to males with persistent PSA?

options.text: When the PSA is rising and results will influence subsequent treatment decisions
Only after conventional imaging has been performed first
As a mandatory baseline investigation before any treatment
When PSA levels exceed 10 ng/mL regardless of treatment intent

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that PSMA PET/CT should be offered to males with persistent and rising PSA if the results will influence subsequent treatment decisions. There is no mention of conventional imaging requirements, mandatory baseline status, or specific PSA thresholds.

questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the minimum number of PSA measurements required for calculating PSA doubling time (PSA-DT)?
questions.explanation: The guideline states that for PSA-DT calculation, at least three PSA measurements are required. A minimum period of four weeks between measurements is preferable to avoid statistical 'noise' when PSA values are obtained too close together.

Prostate-specific antigen velocity is easier to calculate by subtracting the initial value from the final value, dividing by time. questions.explanation: The guideline states that PSA-DT has been linked with metastasis-free and overall survival in nmCRPC and identifies patients with high-risk nmCRPC who benefit from intensified therapy with a PSA-DT threshold of less than 10 months.

It requires calculating the exponential rise using the natural logarithm of two
It must integrate all PSA values using the MSKCC regression method
It can only be calculated when PSA values are obtained at exactly four-week intervals

questions.explanation: The text states that 'PSAV is easier to calculate by subtracting the initial value from the final value, dividing by time. However, by ignoring middle values, not all PSA values are accurately taken into account.'
questions.section_title: 6.4.2. Controversies in the definitions of clinically relevant PSA relapse
questions.source_text: Between 27% and 53% of all patients undergoing RP or RT develop a rising PSA (PSA recurrence). Whilst metastatic progression is universally preceded by rising PSA levels, physicians must inform the patient that the natural history of PSA-only recurrence may be prolonged and that a measurable PSA may not necessarily lead to clinically apparent metastatic disease. Physicians treating patients with PSA-only recurrence face a difficult set of decisions in attempting to delay the onset of metastatic

Values must be compared against a baseline measurement taken before any treatment

questions.explanation: The text states that at least three PSA measurements are required, a minimum period of four weeks between measurements is preferable, and all PSA values should be obtained within the past twelve months. questions.question: According to the guideline, what is the PSA threshold that best predicts further metastases after radical prostatectomy (RP)?

options.text: PSA > 2 ng/mL above the nadir value
PSA > 0.2 ng/mL with a doubling time <12 months

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states that after RP, the threshold that best predicts further metastases is a PSA > 0.4 ng/mL and rising. The other options either refer to different treatment contexts (option B applies to primary RT) or are not mentioned in the text.

Three consecutive rises above the lowest PSA value

PSA-DT > 1 year OR pathological ISUP grade group < 4
Interval to biochemical failure > 18 months AND PSA-DT > 1 year

questions.section_title: 6.4.4. The role of imaging in PSA-only recurrence
questions.source_text: Once a PSA recurrence has been diagnosed, it is important to determine whether the recurrence has developed at local or distant sites. A SR and meta-analysis investigated the impact of BCR on clinical endpoints and concluded that patients experiencing BCR are at an increased risk of developing distant metastases, as well as PCa-specific and overall mortality [994]. The effect size of BCR as a risk factor for mortality, however, is highly variable. After primary RP, its impact ranges from HR 1.03

questions.question: According to the systematic review, which of the following is a prognostic factor for distant metastatic recurrence in patients with BCR after RP that is NOT part of the EAU risk stratification criteria?
questions.explanation: The systematic review states that positive surgical margins is associated with distant metastatic recurrence. However, the EAU risk stratification for BCR after RP only uses PSA-DT and pathological ISUP grade group. Positive surgical margins, while prognostic, is not part of the EAU risk categorization.

questions.question: According to the guidelines, what is the approximate positivity rate of PSMA PET/CT in patients with biochemically recurrent prostate cancer when PSA is greater than 2 ng/mL?
questions.explanation: The text states that the positivity rate increases with PSA, from 48% at PSA of 0.2-0.5 ng/ml to >90% at PSA >2 ng/ml.
questions.source_text: 6.4.4.a.1. Bone scan and abdominopelvic CT As BCR after RP or RT precedes clinical metastases by seven to eight years on average [939,1022], the diagnostic yield of conventional imaging techniques (bone scan and abdominopelvic CT) is low in asymptomatic patients [1023]. In men with PSA-only recurrence after RP, the probability of a positive bone scan is < 5% when the PSA level is < 7ng/mL [1024,1025]. Only 11–14% of patients with BCR after RP have a positive CT [1024].

questions.question: Which of the following limitations is explicitly mentioned for 18F-NaF PET/CT in the evaluation of biochemically recurrent prostate cancer?

options.text: High radiation exposure compared to bone scan
Lower sensitivity than conventional bone scan
Lack of specificity and inability to assess soft-tissue metastases

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 18F-NaF PET/CT 'is limited by a relative lack of specificity and by the fact that it does not assess soft-tissue metastases.'
questions.source_text: 6.4.4.a.1. Bone scan and abdominopelvic CT As BCR after RP or RT precedes clinical metastases by seven to eight years on average [939,1022], the diagnostic yield of conventional imaging techniques (bone scan and abdominopelvic CT) is low in asymptomatic patients [1023]. In men with PSA-only recurrence after RP, the probability of a positive bone scan is < 5% when the PSA level is < 7ng/mL [1024,1025]. Only 11–14% of patients with BCR after RP have a positive CT [1024].

questions.question: What is the PSA threshold for MRI positivity in detecting local recurrence after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The guideline text states that 'The PSA threshold for MRI positivity appears to be between 0.3 and 0.5ng/mL' when detecting local recurrences in the prostatic bed after radical prostatectomy.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that after RP, PSMA hybrid imaging is the modality with the highest sensitivity at low PSA levels (< 0.5 ng/mL). This modality may help distinguish patients with recurrences confined to the prostatic fossa from those with distant metastases.
questions.section_title: 6.4.4.c. Summary of evidence of imaging in case of biochemical recurrence
questions.source_text: In patients with BCR, imaging can detect both local recurrences and distant metastases, however, the sensitivity of detection depends on the PSA level. After RP, PSMA hybrid imaging is the modality with the highest sensitivity at low PSA levels (< 0.5ng/mL) and may help distinguishing patients with recurrences confined to the prostatic fossa from those with distant metastases, which may impact the design and use of post-RP SRT. Because distant metastases reduce the cost of treatment
Because post-RT local salvage treatments have substantial morbidity
Because distant metastases increase PSA doubling time

questions.question: According to the Tilki et al. study on salvage radiotherapy (SRT), what was the 15-year overall survival difference between patients who received SRT versus no radiation after radical prostatectomy?
questions.explanation: The Tilki et al. matched-pair analysis of 1,832 patients showed 15-year overall survival rates of 85.3% for SRT versus 74.4% for no RT (p = 0.04). Option B (84.3% vs 76.9%) refers to the metastasis-free survival rates, not overall survival.
questions.section_title: 6.4.5.a. Treatment of PSA-only recurrences after radical prostatectomy
questions.source_text: 6.4.5.a.1. Boorjian et al. reported a 75% reduced risk of systemic progression with SRT when comparing 856 SRT patients with 1,801 non-SRT patients [1054]. questions.question: Based on the studies presented, what post-SRT PSA level is consistently associated with significantly better long-term outcomes (overall survival, distant metastasis, and disease-specific mortality)?
questions.explanation: Multiple studies (Bartkowiak, Jackson, Stish) demonstrated that achieving a post-SRT PSA < 0.1 ng/mL was associated with significantly better outcomes: Bartkowiak showed 5.9-year OS of 98% vs 92% (p=0.005), Jackson showed 5-year DM of 5% vs 29% and DSM of 2% vs 7%, and Stish showed improved MFS and DSMS with PSA ≤ 0.5 ng/mL. The most consistent and significant threshold across these studies was PSA < 0.1 ng/mL.

HIFU demonstrated significantly better RFS compared to brachytherapy

PSA-DT of ≤6 months and PSA ≥1ng/mL above nadir after any treatment modality
Gleason score ≥9 and rapid PSA doubling time regardless of PSA level
Presence of bone metastases on imaging and PSA level ≥5ng/mL

questions.section_title: 6.4.7. Recommendations for second-line therapy after treatment with curative intent
questions.source_text: The aim of hormonal therapy (ADT) in relapsing patients is to improve OS, delay the onset of distant metastases, and maintain QoL. Biochemical response alone to ADT holds no clinical benefit for a patient. An SR including studies published from 2000 onwards [1139], found conflicting results on the clinical effectiveness of ADT after previous curative therapy. questions.question: According to the EMBARK trial results, what was the five-year metastasis-free survival (MFS) in the combination group (enzalutamide plus leuprolide)?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that after a median follow-up of 60.7 months, the five-year MFS was 87.3% (95% CI, 83.0-90.6) in the combination group, compared to 71.4% in the leuprolide-alone group and 80.0% in the monotherapy group.

questions.question: According to the guideline text, what is the preferred target testosterone level for castration in prostate cancer treatment?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the preferred target level should be defined as <20ng/dL (<0.7 nmol/L) because better results are observed with lower testosterone levels compared to the historical 50ng/dL level.
questions.source_text: The castration level of testosterone is < 50ng/dL (1.7 nmol/L), defined more than 40 years ago when testosterone testing was less sensitive. Current methods have shown that the mean value after surgical castration is 15ng/dL [1035]. Therefore, a preferred target level should be defined as < 20ng/dL (< 0.7 nmol/L). This definition is important because better results are repeatedly observed in ADT monotherapy cohorts with lower testosterone levels compared to 50ng

questions.question: What castration level do regulatory authorities and clinical trials currently use?

options.text: The mean surgical castration value of 15ng/dL
An average of the mean and preferred target levels

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the castrate level considered by regulatory authorities and in clinical trials is still the historical <50ng/dL (1.7 nmol/L), even though evidence supports a lower preferred target.

questions.question: According to the guidelines, within what timeframe is castration level typically achieved after bilateral orchiectomy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that bilateral orchiectomy achieves castration level 'usually reached within less than twelve hours.'
questions.source_text: Bilateral orchiectomy or subcapsular pulpectomy is still considered the primary treatment modality for ADT. It is a simple, cheap and low-complication procedure. It is easily performed under local anaesthesia and is the quickest way to achieve a castration level that is usually reached within less than twelve hours. It is irreversible and therefore does not allow for intermittent treatment [1039].

questions.question: When does the testosterone flare following the first LHRH agonist injection typically begin?

options.text: Two to three days after administration

questions.explanation: The text states that the testosterone surge or flare-up phenomenon starts two to three days after administration and lasts approximately one week.
questions.source_text: Long-acting LHRH agonists are currently the main stay of ADT. These synthetic analogues of LHRH are administered as depot injections on a monthly, three-monthly, six-monthly or yearly schedule. The first injection induces a transient rise in luteinising hormone (LH) and follicle-stimulating hormone (FSH), leading to the ‘testosterone surge’ or ‘flare-up’ phenomenon, which starts two to three days after administration and lasts for approximately one week. This transient hormonal rise has historic

options.text: It completely eliminates the risk of flare
It decreases the incidence of clinical flare but does not completely remove the risk
It has no effect on the clinical flare
It increases the severity of the flare

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that concomitant therapy with an anti-androgen decreases the incidence of clinical flare but does not completely remove the risk. It also notes that more recent analyses question the clinical relevance of this testosterone flare in most patients.

questions.question: Which of the following side effects is most commonly associated with non-steroidal anti-androgen monotherapy?
questions.explanation: According to the guideline, gynaecomastia occurs in 70% of patients receiving non-steroidal anti-androgen monotherapy. Cardiovascular toxicity and hepatotoxicity are mentioned as side effects, but gynaecomastia is the most common. Prostatic bleeding is not mentioned in the text.
questions.source_text: These oral compounds are classified according to their chemical structure as: steroidal, e.g. cyproterone acetate (CPA), megestrol acetate and medroxyprogesterone acetate; and non-steroidal or pure, e.g. nilutamide, flutamide and bicalutamide. Both classes compete with androgens at the receptor level. This leads to an unchanged or slightly elevated testosterone level. questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that BRCA mutations predispose patients to PCa whilst also making tumours more responsive to PARPi, and therefore BRCA status should be assessed for optimal treatment selection.

questions.explanation: The text directly states that the interaction between BRCA and PARP is a form of synthetic lethal effect, meaning that simultaneously functional loss of two genes leads to cell death, while a defect in any single gene only has a limited effect on cell viability.

questions.question: Which molecule is targeted by pembrolizumab?
questions.explanation: Pembrolizumab is a PD-1 inhibitor. Checkpoint inhibitors target CTLA4, PD-1, and PD-L1, with pembrolizumab specifically targeting PD-1.
questions.source_text: Checkpoint inhibitors target the molecules CTLA4, programmed cell death protein 1 (PD-1), and programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1). For advanced PCa patients who are microsatellite instability-high/deficient mismatch repair (MSI-H/ dMMR ), the PD-1 inhibitor pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA but not by the EMA. The label is tumour agnostic [1186,1187].

questions.question: For advanced prostate cancer patients with MSI-H/dMMR, what is the approval status of pembrolizumab?

options.text: Approved by both FDA and EMA
Approved by neither FDA nor EMA

questions.explanation: The text states that pembrolizumab has been approved by the FDA but not by the EMA for advanced PCa patients with MSI-H/dMMR.

questions.question: What is the primary mechanism of action of radiopharmaceutical therapy (RPT)?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'The mechanism of action for RPT is radiation-induced killing of cells.' The other options describe different therapeutic mechanisms not mentioned in the guideline text.
questions.source_text: Radiopharmaceutical therapy (RPT) is based on the delivery of radioactive isotopes to tumour-associated targets. The mechanism of action for RPT is radiation-induced killing of cells. Radionuclides with different emission properties are used to deliver radiation. The most commonly used radionuclides are represented by β-particles (e.g. 177 Lu) or α-particles (e.g. 223 Ra, 225 Ac). 223 Ra based on its biochemical similarity to Calcium, is integrated in bones with increased osteoblastic activity,

questions.question: Which radionuclide property results in minimal toxic effects on adjacent healthy tissue?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'The short path of the β-particles (0.05–0.08 mm) results in minimal toxic effects in adjacent healthy tissue.' The other properties described are correct for different radionuclide characteristics but are not associated with minimizing toxic effects.

questions.question: What cellular processes are promoted by the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway?

options.text: Cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth
Cell differentiation, embryonic development, and tissue repair
Cellular respiration, ion transport, and protein synthesis

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway is central for promoting cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth.
questions.source_text: AKT inhibitors are orally administered ATP-competitive molecules that target the three AKT isoforms. AKT propagates signals from the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway which is central for promoting cell survival, proliferation, metabolism, and growth. AKT inhibitors interrupt key growth and survival signals in cancer cells by locking AKT into an inactive state and preventing downstream phosphorylation.

Enhancing NK cell activity and stimulating anti-tumor immunity
Blocking topoisomerase and inducing DNA strand breaks
Inhibiting angiogenesis and cutting off tumor blood supply

questions.explanation: The guideline text directly states in Table 6.6.2 that patients with PSA <0.2 ng/mL after 7 months of ADT have a median survival of 75 months. The 50-month figure refers to overall median survival for synchronous mHSPC, not specifically to the PSA prognostic group.

questions.question: According to CHAARTED criteria, which of the following defines HIGH volume metastatic disease?

options.text: Two bone metastases limited to the pelvis
≥4 bone metastases including at least one outside the vertebral column or pelvis, and/or visceral metastasis
Single bone metastasis with PSA >10 ng/mL
3 bone metastases all within the pelvis without visceral involvement

questions.explanation: The guideline text directly defines high volume in CHAARTED as ≥4 bone metastases including ≥1 outside the vertebral column or pelvis AND/OR visceral metastasis. Lymph nodes are explicitly noted as not considered visceral metastases. A single bone metastasis, even with high PSA, does not meet the volume threshold.

questions.question: In terms of which outcomes is older generation NSAA monotherapy considered inferior to ADT?

options.text: Overall survival (OS), clinical progression, treatment failure, and treatment discontinuation due to AEs
Cost-effectiveness, patient satisfaction, and quality of life metrics
Biochemical recurrence rates and PSA doubling time
Surgical complications and hospital readmission rates

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that older generation NSAA monotherapy is inferior to ADT in terms of OS, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to AEs.
questions.source_text: Older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy is inferior to ADT (either medical or surgical) in terms of OS, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to AEs [1227] and is generally not recommended. For combination treatment of mHSPC ADT is the standard of care.

questions.question: For combination treatment of metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer (mHSPC), what is the standard of care according to the guidelines?
questions.explanation: The guideline clearly states that for combination treatment of mHSPC, ADT is the standard of care.
questions.source_text: Older generation non-steroidal anti-androgen (NSAA) monotherapy is inferior to ADT (either medical or surgical) in terms of OS, clinical progression, treatment failure and treatment discontinuation due to AEs [1227] and is generally not recommended. For combination treatment of mHSPC ADT is the standard of care.

questions.source_text: Three independent reviews [1228-1230] and two meta-analyses [1231,1232] looked at the clinical efficacy of intermittent androgen deprivation (IAD) therapy. The trial was inconclusive; inferior survival with IAD cannot be completely ruled out
IAD and continuous ADT showed equivalent survival with statistical significance

questions.question: According to the 2019 Cochrane analysis, what did early ADT probably extend in patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive prostate cancer?

options.text: Time to death of any cause and time to death from PCa
Progression-free survival without affecting overall survival
Time to castration resistance and quality of life scores

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that the 2019 Cochrane analysis concluded early ADT probably extends 'time to death of any cause and time to death from PCa'. The analysis included only a very limited number of metastatic patients.
questions.section_title: 6.6.4.c. Early versus deferred androgen deprivation therapy
questions.source_text: Early treatment before the onset of symptoms is recommended in the majority of patients with metastatic hormone-sensitive disease. A Cochrane analysis from 2019 regarding the topic concluded that early ADT probably extends time to death of any cause and time to death from PCa [1234], but the analysis included only a very limited number of metastatic patients. Randomised phase III data is lacking in this specific setting and specifically not with the combination therapies that are standard nowada

Systematic reviews of early versus delayed ADT

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'Randomised phase III data is lacking in this specific setting and specifically not with the combination therapies that are standard nowadays.' The evidence gap identified is specifically regarding randomised controlled trial data with modern combination therapies.

options.text: As standard first-line treatment for all newly diagnosed prostate cancer patients
In patients with impending spinal cord compression or other potential impending complications
In patients with bone metastases only
As maintenance therapy after completion of radiation treatment

questions.explanation: The guideline text explicitly states that testosterone is reduced much faster with orchiectomy and LHRH antagonists, therefore they are recommended in patients with impending spinal cord compression or other potential impending complications.

questions.question: What is the magnitude of the survival advantage provided by combined androgen blockade using a NSAA compared to monotherapy?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that combined androgen blockade using a NSAA provides a small survival advantage (< 5%) versus monotherapy.
questions.section_title: 6.6.5.a.‘Combined’ androgen blockade with older generation NSAA (bicalutamide, flutamide, nilutamide)
questions.source_text: Systematic reviews have shown that combined androgen blockade using a NSAA appears to provide a small survival advantage (< 5%) versus monotherapy (surgical castration or LHRH agonists) [1236,1237]. This minimal survival advantage must be balanced against the increased side effects, especially because the newer combination therapies are more effective as shown specifically for enzalutamide, which was tested against NSAA in phase III trials [1238,1239].

questions.question: In phase III trials, against which agent was enzalutamide specifically tested?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that enzalutamide was tested against NSAA in phase III trials, showing that newer combination therapies are more effective.

questions.question: According to the PEACE-1 trial, in which patient group did adding prostate radiation therapy (RT) to standard of care (SOC) plus an androgen receptor pathway inhibitor (ARPI) significantly prolong progression-free survival?

options.text: All patients with mHSPC regardless of metastatic volume
Patients with low-volume metastatic disease treated with SOC + ARPI

questions.explanation: The PEACE-1 trial demonstrated that adding prostate RT (74Gy in 37 fractions) to SOC + ARPI significantly prolonged PFS from 4.4 years to 7.5 years specifically in the low-volume metastatic burden group.
questions.source_text: The first reported trial evaluating prostate RT in men with mHSPC was the HORRAD trial. Four hundred and thirty-two patients were randomised to ADT alone or ADT plus IMRT with IGRT to the prostate. Overall survival was not significantly different (HR: 0.9 [0.7–1.14]) and median time to PSA progression was significantly improved in the RT arm (HR: 0.78 [0.63–0.97]) [1271]. questions.explanation: The STAMPEDE trial confirmed that RT to the primary tumour did not improve OS in unselected patients. However, in the low-volume subgroup (n=819), there was a significant OS benefit with HR: 0.64 for OS improvement.

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that 'ADT-free (pADT) survival was the primary endpoint in the STOMP study which was longer with MDT than with surveillance.'

MDT did not show any significant benefit when added to systemic treatment
MDT improved PFS only when combined with ADT alone, not with ADT plus ARPI

This significant benefit was observed both in the patient group receiving ADT alone or ADT + ARPI.'

options.text: Three consecutive rises in PSA at least one week apart resulting in two 50% increases over the nadir, and a PSA > 2 ng/mL
Two consecutive rises in PSA at least one week apart with a PSA > 1 ng/mL
A single PSA elevation above the previous nadir value
PSA doubling over a three-month period regardless of absolute value

questions.explanation: The guideline explicitly states biochemical progression requires three consecutive rises in PSA at least one week apart, resulting in two 50% increases over the nadir, with a minimum PSA level > 2 ng/mL.
questions.source_text: Castrate serum testosterone < 50 ng/dL or 1.7 nmol/L plus either: a.Biochemical progression: Three consecutive rises in PSA at least one week apart resulting in two 50% increases over the nadir, and a PSA > 2 ng/mL. b.Radiological progression: The appearance of new lesions: either two or more new bone lesions on bone scan, ideally confirmed [1290], or a soft tissue lesion using RECIST (Response Evaluation Criteria in Solid Tumours) [1291]. Symptomatic progression alone

Enlargement of existing metastatic lesions on CT scan

questions.question: According to the RADAR consensus statement, at what PSA level should a bone scan and CT scan first be performed in asymptomatic men treated with ADT who are being monitored for metastatic disease?
questions.explanation: The RADAR consensus statement suggests a bone scan and CT scan when the PSA reaches 2 ng/mL. If this is negative, it should be repeated when the PSA reaches 5 ng/mL.

questions.explanation: The SPARTAN trial evaluated apalutamide versus placebo in patients with nmCRPC (M0 CRPC). The PROSPER trial tested enzalutamide and the ARAMIS trial tested darolutamide. Abiraterone acetate was not studied in these three trials.

questions.question: According to the PROpel trial, what was the primary endpoint used to demonstrate the efficacy of abiraterone/prednisone plus olaparib in the first-line treatment of mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The PROpel trial used imaging-based progression-free survival (ibPFS) as the primary endpoint. The combination showed significantly improved ibPFS of 24.8 versus 16.6 months (HR 0.66; p=0.001) compared to placebo. Overall survival was a key secondary endpoint but did not meet statistical significance at the prespecified analysis.

questions.explanation: The text states that rPFS was longer with rucaparib than with docetaxel (11.2 months vs. 8.3 months) with a hazard ratio of 0.53 (95% CI, 0.37-0.77).

Prior treatment with a PARP inhibitor

questions.explanation: Patients were selected for disease progression after treatment with an ARPI (androgen receptor pathway inhibitor), not after a PARP inhibitor. Prior treatment with a PARP inhibitor was not an inclusion criterion.

questions.explanation: The updated analysis reported a median rPFS of 11.60 months in the ¹⁷⁷Lu‑PSMA‑617 group versus 5.59 months in the ARPI‑change group.

questions.explanation: The trial states that 141/234 participants (60.3%) randomised to the ARPI change crossed over to receive ¹⁷⁷Lu‑PSMA‑617.

questions.question: In the TROPIC trial, what was the dose comparison evaluated for cabazitaxel in the second-line setting?
questions.explanation: The text states that in post-marketing phase III trials, 20 mg/m² cabazitaxel was not inferior to 25 mg/m² in terms of OS in the second-line setting, but was less toxic. Therefore, the lower dose should be preferred.
questions.section_title: 6.7.8. Treatment after docetaxel and one line of ARPI for mCRPC
questions.source_text: All patients who receive treatment for mCRPC will eventually progress. All treatment options in this setting are presented in Table 6.7.3. There is a paucity of high-level data with regards to the sequence of treatments, particularly in case of pretreatment for mHSPC with ARPI and/or docetaxel or other agents. questions.question: What was the primary endpoint and key result of the COU-AA-301 trial evaluating abiraterone acetate after docetaxel for mCRPC?
questions.explanation: The COU-AA-301 trial had overall survival as its primary endpoint. After a median follow-up of 20.2 months, median survival in the abiraterone acetate group was 15.8 months compared to 11.2 months in the placebo arm (HR: 0.74, p < 0.0001).

questions.question: According to the CARD trial, what was the reduction in risk of death with cabazitaxel compared to another ARPI in patients progressing within 12 months on previous abiraterone or enzalutamide?
questions.explanation: The text states that cabazitaxel reduced the risk of death by 36% vs. ARPI in the CARD trial for patients progressing quickly on AR targeted therapy.
questions.source_text: For men progressing quickly on AR targeted therapy (< twelve months) it is now clear that cabazitaxel is the treatment supported by the best data. The CARD trial, an open label randomised phase III trial, evaluated cabazitaxel after docetaxel and one line of ARPI (either AAP or enzalutamide) [1293]. It included patients progressing in less than twelve months on previous abiraterone or enzalutamide for mCRPC. Cabazitaxel more than doubled rPFS vs. another ARPI and reduced the risk of deat

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT recommended based on evidence of cross-resistance?

options.text: Sequential use of enzalutamide followed by abiraterone
Sequential use of apalutamide followed by darolutamide
Sequential use of radium-223 and cabazitaxel
Sequential use of enzalutamide and apalutamide

questions.explanation: The text explicitly states that sequential use of the lutamides (apalutamide, darolutamide, enzalutamide) is not recommended as their mode of action is very similar. Evidence of cross-resistance between enzalutamide and abiraterone should be extrapolated to all these agents.

questions.question: Which PSMA therapeutic radiopharmaceutical is supported by the most robust data according to the guidelines?
questions.explanation: The text explicitly states 'The PSMA therapeutic radiopharmaceutical supported by the most robust data is 177Lu-PSMA-617.'
